snippets.title: Minimal `launch.json` (VS Code)
snippets.explanation: Commit this to `.vscode/launch.json`; the whole team gets the same debug profiles. `pathMappings` is essential when attaching to a containerized process.

snippets.title: Enable debugpy in a remote / container process
snippets.code:
# In your entrypoint (guarded so it only runs when explicitly enabled):
import os
if os.environ.get("DEBUG_WAIT") == "1":
    import debugpy
    debugpy.listen(("0.0.0.0", 5678))
    print("Waiting for debugger to attach on :5678 ...", flush=True)
    debugpy.wait_for_client()
    print("Debugger attached, resuming.")

from ragapp.pipeline import main
main()
snippets.explanation: `debugpy.listen` opens a port; `wait_for_client()` blocks until the IDE attaches. Guard with an env var so production runs aren't accidentally blocked.

snippets.title: Forward the port from k8s / Docker
snippets.code:
# Kubernetes:
kubectl port-forward pod/ragapp-7d9 5678:5678

# Docker (running a single container):
# docker run -p 5678:5678 -e DEBUG_WAIT=1 ragapp

# Then in VS Code: Run and Debug → 'Attach (localhost:5678)'
snippets.explanation: Combined with the `debugpy.listen` snippet above, this is a 60-second setup for live remote debugging.

snippets.title: Logpoint syntax (VS Code)
snippets.code:
Right-click in the gutter → Add Logpoint...
Message:    doc {doc.id} entering reranker (score={doc.score:.2f})

# Logpoints don't pause execution — they print to the Debug Console.
# Like a temporary `logger.info()` that you don't have to commit & revert.
snippets.explanation: Logpoints are the single biggest win over `print` debugging — interactive, removable, no source-tree pollution.

snippets.title: Programmatic breakpoint for `pytest --pdb` users
snippets.code:
import debugpy

# Wait for the IDE to attach the first time this runs:
debugpy.listen(("127.0.0.1", 5678))
debugpy.wait_for_client()
debugpy.breakpoint()        # equivalent to a gutter breakpoint
snippets.explanation: Useful when you want a programmatic 'always-pause-here' that an IDE will catch — e.g. inside a slow integration test.
